rationale_for_baseline_tier: Mild, uncomplicated antibiotic-associated oral candidiasis — afebrile, patent airway, no deep retrosternal odynophagia, no dysphagia. It is telehealth/GP-manageable with a topical antifungal and analgesia. The reason it sits at T2 (prompt review, 24-48 hours) rather than T0/T1 is the pain-driven fluid restriction with a moderate dehydration risk in a person living alone, plus the need for a prescription antifungal — the patient should be seen/treated promptly. It is NOT T3+: there is no unexcludable serious mimic here (oesophageal extension was actively screened and is absent). The source note's 'urgent after-hours home visit' framing reflects MBS billing justification, not true clinical acuity, and must not drive the tier upward.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New deep retrosternal / central chest pain on swallowing (odynophagia)
escalation_edges.rationale: Deep pain on swallowing suggests oesophageal candidiasis extension, which needs systemic antifungal therapy, in-person assessment and consideration of an underlying immunocompromising condition.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start getting pain deep in the centre of your chest when you swallow, go to an emergency department or urgent care the same day — it can mean the infection has spread down your food pipe and needs stronger treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to drink fluids at all, or signs of dehydration (dizziness on standing, no urine passed for more than 8 hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: Pain-driven inability to maintain hydration can progress to clinical dehydration; living alone, there is no one to detect deterioration, lowering the threshold to escalate.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down, or you feel dizzy standing up or haven't passed urine for more than 8 hours, go to an emergency department — you may be getting dehydrated and need fluids.
escalation_edges.contextual_modification: Lower threshold because the patient lives alone — encourage them to tell a friend or neighbour and to have someone check on them.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever or systemic upset is not a feature of mild localised oral candidiasis and suggests more extensive or invasive infection needing same-day assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever or start feeling generally unwell, see a doctor urgently the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Difficulty breathing, throat swelling, drooling or a muffled voice
escalation_edges.rationale: Any sign of airway compromise is an immediate life threat, however unlikely in mild oral thrush — a conservative catch-all escalation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your throat starts to swell, you drool, your voice becomes muffled, or you have any trouble breathing — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, currently restricting fluids because of oral pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for escalating on hydration; explicitly ask whether someone can check on the patient, and advise them to contact a friend, neighbour or family member while symptoms limit their intake.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can manage this at home with the prescribed antifungal mouth medicine and regular pain relief so you can keep sipping cool, bland fluids. Arrange a prompt telehealth or GP appointment (within 24-48 hours) to get the antifungal and to have your mouth reviewed, and book a follow-up in about 5-7 days to make sure it is clearing.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Your regular GP or a telehealth service for the antifungal script; for after-hours health advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice. Go to an emergency department only if a red flag appears — severe pain deep in the chest on swallowing, you cannot drink at all, you become dizzy or stop passing urine, or you develop a high fever. Call 000 for any breathing difficulty or throat swelling.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP about the recent broad-spectrum antibiotic course that preceded this, and mention if this has ever happened before — recurrent episodes warrant screening for an underlying cause.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot perform the scrape test or KOH microscopy to definitively confirm candidiasis versus leukoplakia, and cannot directly examine the oropharynx/airway or objectively assess hydration status.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as presumptive oral candidiasis given the classic post-antibiotic history and video-visible curd-like plaques, start topical antifungal therapy, and safety-net clearly for oesophageal extension and dehydration; recommend in-person review only if it does not improve, the picture is atypical, or a red flag emerges.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either over-triaging a benign, telehealth-manageable condition to urgent in-person care or ED because the source framing sounded alarming, OR reassuring the patient without safety-netting the two things that could genuinely turn this serious (oesophageal extension and dehydration).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Assigning pure self-care with no medical review — missing the need for a prescription antifungal and, worse, omitting safety-netting for oesophageal extension and dehydration.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient, living alone, cannot maintain hydration, deteriorates without anyone present, or an oesophageal extension progresses unrecognised.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a mild, telehealth-manageable oral thrush to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — over-triage driven by the source note's inflated urgency framing, causing resource waste and patient anxiety.
